coredata.dc:title: Improving the surface area of metal organic framework-derived porous carbon through constructing inner support by compatible graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Tingting
Yuan, Renlu
Guo, Nannan
Zhu, Jiayao
Gan, Xuemeng
Li, Qiqi
Qin, Fuwei
Luo, Wanxia
Wang, Luxiang
Zhang, Su
Song, Huaihe
Jia, Dianzeng
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as promising precursors to prepare porous carbons due to their unique coordination structure with abundant pores and various chemical compositions. However, the structural collapse and pore shrinkage during pyrolysis severely decrease the surface area of the prepared porous carbons. Herein, we propose an inner support strategy to prepare MOF-derived carbons with improved surface area using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the compatible frameworks. GQDs with abundant carboxyl groups (–COOH) and rigid structure can uniformly distribute in MOF-5 precursor by coordinating with [Zn4O]6+ clusters and effectively reinforce the carbon skeleton during pyrolysis. Therefore, the rational GQDs embedded MOF-5 derived porous carbon (GMPC-0.35) shows greatly improved specific surface area (1841 m2 g−1) and mesopore volume (1.62 cm3 g−1) than pure MOF-5 derived carbon (1358 m2 g−1, 0.59 cm3 g−1). As an application exemplification, GMPC-0.35 performs high specific capacitance of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and good capacitance retention of 53% at 100 A g−1 as the electrode material for supercapacitors, which are higher than most of the reported MOF-5 derived carbons. Therefore, the compatible GQDs support is promising for preparing functional MOF-derived carbon materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Graphene quantum dots
Mesoporous carbon
Inner support
Supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation and structural transformation process of MPC and GMPC-0.35.
HRTEM images of (a) MOF-5 and (b) G-MOF-5. (c) XRD patterns, (d) XPS survey spectra, (e) Zn2p, and (f) C1s spectra of MOF-5 and G-MOF-5.
HRTEM images of (a) and (b) MPC, (c) and (d) GMPC-0.35.

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (b) pore size distributions, (c) specific surface areas and porous structure evolution, (d) XRD patterns, (e) Raman spectra, and (f) electric conductivity of MPC and GMPCs.

Electrochemical performance of the samples measured by three-electrode system: (a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1, (b) GCD profiles at 1 A g−1, (c) rate performance, (d) electrochemical kinetics parameters obtained from the Nyquist plots, and (e) Bode plots of MPC and GMPCs. (f) Schematic illustration of ion-transport and charge-transfer in GMPCs.

Electrochemical performance of GMPC-0.35//GMPC-0.35 symmetric supercapacitor: (a) GCD profiles at different current densities, (b) IR drop, (c) Ragone plots compared with other reported MOF-5 derived carbon supercapacitors, and (d) cycle performance at 10 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Improving the surface area of metal organic framework-derived porous carbon through constructing inner support by compatible graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry
Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials
Autonomous Region Institute of Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors with equal contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The highly compatible graphene quantum dots construct rigid inner support in MOF-5, enabling the derived carbon with improved surface area, abundant mesoporous channels, good conductivity, as well as improved capacitive performance.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as promising precursors to prepare porous carbons due to their unique coordination structure with abundant pores and various chemical compositions. However, the structural collapse and pore shrinkage during pyrolysis severely decrease the surface area of the prepared porous carbons. Herein, we propose an inner support strategy to prepare MOF-derived carbons with improved surface area using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the compatible frameworks. GQDs with abundant carboxyl groups (–COOH) and rigid structure can uniformly distribute in MOF-5 precursor by coordinating with [Zn4O]6+ clusters and effectively reinforce the carbon skeleton during pyrolysis. Therefore, the rational GQDs embedded MOF-5 derived porous carbon (GMPC-0.35) shows greatly improved specific surface area (1841 m2 g−1) and mesopore volume (1.62 cm3 g−1) than pure MOF-5 derived carbon (1358 m2 g−1, 0.59 cm3 g−1). As an application exemplification, GMPC-0.35 performs high specific capacitance of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and good capacitance retention of 53% at 100 A g−1 as the electrode material for supercapacitors, which are higher than most of the reported MOF-5 derived carbons. Therefore, the compatible GQDs support is promising for preparing functional MOF-derived carbon materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), constructed from the spacing assembly of metal ions/clusters with organic ligands by coordination bonds [1,2], have received wide attention in the fields of gas adsorption and storage, chemical separations, catalysis, light harvesting, and energy storage due to their fascinating physiochemical characteristics [3-7]. Taking advantage of the structural diversity, selectable elemental species, high specific surface area, and ordered porosity, MOFs have emerged as promising precursors to prepare various functional porous carbons [8-11]. Generally, MOF-derived porous carbons can achieve designable morphology and in-situ doped heteroatoms (e.g., N, P, S, and B) by selecting appropriate MOF precursors [12,13]. The organic ligands serve as the carbon source, and the uniformly distributed metal ions show effects on crystallization and formation of porous structure [14-17]. However, the unstable spacing coordination structure of MOFs usually causes a structure collapse during pyrolysis, resulting in severe pore shrinkage and decreased surface area [18-22]. Thus, preparation of MOF-derived porous carbons with robust skeleton and high surface area is still challenging.

To overcome the aforementioned problem, a common strategy is introducing second carbon sources in the pores of MOFs [23-25]. For example, Xu et al. [26,27] prepared a nanocarbon with large surface area of 2872 m2 g−1 and high pore volume of 2.06 cm3 g−1 by introducing furfuryl alcohol vapor into the pores of MOF-5 and following polymerization and carbonization. Zhong et al. [28] introduced carbon sources, such as xylitol, sucrose, melamine, and urea, into the pores of ZIF-8. The relevant co-derived porous carbons showed significantly improved specific surface areas and pore volumes (934–2159 m2 g−1, 0.5–1.4 cm3 g−1) than the ZIF-8 derived carbon (603 m2 g−1, 0.3 cm3 g−1). However, the amount and distribution of additional carbon source is hard to control, and the excessive infiltration of secondary carbon precursors may result in completely blocked pores and decreased surface area of the MOF-derived carbons [29].

Constructing support framework by high-aspect-ratio graphene is another attractive strategy because these nanocarbons can simultaneously improve the conductivity of the products. Recent reports mainly focus on designing the outer support frameworks to alleviate the particle aggregation and/or to construct stable mesoporous structures. Fan et al. [30] reported a novel porous carbon with “brick-and-mortar” structure by carbonizing sandwiched MOF-5/graphene oxides hybrid materials. Graphene sheets act as the structural scaffolds for supporting MOF-5 derived porous carbon film, leading to wider pore size distribution (1–8 nm) than pure MOF-5 derived carbon. Li et al. [31] prepared MOF-derived porous carbon by in-situ growth of ZIF-8 and ZIF-67 on graphene oxide and subsequent thermal treatment. The graphene serves as the scaffold to support the uniform MOF-derived carbon and achieve high specific surface area of 560 m2 g−1 with a broad pore size distribution ranging from 0.7 to 35 nm. Developing stable MOF precursors with inner support by rational nanomaterials may be a promising approach to prepare novel MOF-derived porous carbons with thermodynamic stability, high surface area, and tailorable pore texture [32-34]. However, using nanocarbons as the inner supports to inhibit the structure collapse and pore shrinkage of MOFs has barely been reported before because most large-sized nanocarbons show poor compatibility with MOF precursors, which are hard to form uniformly distributed inner support on a microscale.

Herein, we develop an inner support strategy to intensify the structural stability of MOF-derived porous carbon using MOF-5 as an exemplified precursor and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the compatible and rigid scaffold. The small-sized GQDs have abundant carboxyl groups (–COOH), which can preferentially coordinate with metal clusters [Zn4O]6+, similar as the coordination between bezene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid (H2BDC) and [Zn4O]6+ in MOF-5. The uniformly introduced GQDs effectively avoid the structure collapse and pore shrinkage during pyrolysis, leading to the derived porous carbon (GMPC-0.35) possessing much higher specific surface area (1841 m2 g−1) and mesopore volume (1.62 cm3 g−1) than the conventional MOF-5 derived porous carbon (MPC, 1358 m2 g−1, 0.59 cm3 g−1). Moreover, the highly crystallized GQDs also construct entire conductive networks in GMPCs, contributing to an improved conductivity from 60.7 to 112.5 S m−1. When used as the electrode material for supercapacitors, the optimized GMPC-0.35 shows an improved specific capacitance of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and remarkable rate performance with 53% capacitance retention at 100 A g−1, revealing their structure merits originated from the novel GQDs inner support.
GQDs were prepared by acid oxidation method using coal powder (Heishan, Xinjiang, China) as the precursor. The details are given in our previous studies [35,36].

MOF-5 was prepared through a solvothermal method [37]. GQD embedded MOF-5 (G-MOF-5) was synthesized by the same process with the addition of GQDs. In a typical synthesis process, 1.15 g H2BDC and 5.95 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved into 80 mL N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF). Then 0.40 g GQDs were fully dispersed into the above mixture solution by stirring and sonication to form a homogeneous dispersion. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL) and kept at 120 °C for 14 h. After cooling down to room temperature, G-MOF-5 was collected by washing with DMF and methanol for several times to remove the unreacted sources, then dried at 120 °C in vacuum overnight.

MPC and GMPC-0.35 was obtained by carbonizing MOF-5 and G-MOF-5, respectively, at 900 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere with the heating rate 2 °C min- 1. After that, the obtained sample were first immersed into HCl solution (1 mol L-1) to remove inorganic salts, and further washed to neutral using deionized water. To study the influence of GQD addition ratio on the properties of the derived carbon, GMPC-x, where “x” stands for the mass ratio of GQDs to H2BDC, were prepared by the same synthesis and pyrolysis process but with different GQDs dosage.

The morphology and structure of the samples were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800), high revolution translation electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advances), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Xi), Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA). The surface area and pore volume were measured by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics) based on the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method. The pore size distribution was obtained by density functional theory method [38-40].

The working electrodes were prepared as follows: the sample, conductive carbon black, and polytetrafluoroethylene binder with the mass ratio of 85: 10: 5 were uniformly mixed in ethanol. Then, the slurry was coated onto nickel foam (1 × 1 cm2) and vacuum dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The mass loading of the active material in one electrode is ca. 2 mg cm- 2. The electrodes were tested by three- and two- electrode systems in KOH aqueous electrolyte (6 mol L-1) according to our previous works [41]. The electric conductivity, cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements were performed on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China).

The preparation and structural transformation process of MPC and GMPC-0.35 are illustrated in Fig. 1 . MOF-5 is prepared by spacing coordination of metal clusters [Zn4O]6+ and organic ligands H2BDC. It shows a cubic block appearance with the size of ca. 15 μm (SEM image in Figure S1a). As observed from the HRTEM image (Fig. 2 a), MOF-5 shows a long-range-ordered crystal structure with a typical lattice parameter of ca. 0.28 nm, which is indexed to the (400) crystal face. Nanosized GQDs (ca. 3.2 nm) [35] show good compatibility to MOF-5 because the enriched edge carboxyl groups [42,43] can also coordinate with [Zn4O]6+ (as seen below). Therefore, when introduced into the reaction, they play as heterogeneous nucleation centers as well as spacers, making G-MOF-5 with a polycrystalline structure. As clearly demonstrated from the SEM (Figure S1b) and HRTEM image (Fig. 2b), G-MOF-5 show similar morphology and particle size with MOF-5 (the size of ca. 20 μm), yet they are constructed by differently oriented nanocrystals with the size from 2 to 5 nm.

From the XRD patterns (Fig. 2c), the samples all have four main diffraction peaks located at 6.8°, 9.7°, 13.7°, and 15.4°, corresponding to the (200), (220), (400), and (420) crystal planes of MOF-5, respectively [44-46], indicating that GQDs are uniformly embedded in MOF-5 without disrupting the primary crystal structure. However, the (200) and (400) peaks of G-MOF-5 are more intensive than those of MOF-5. This is because the two-dimensional GQDs acting as the nucleation centers induce the preferential growth of the (200) and (400) planes, which is in well accordance with the HRTEM observed. The XPS analysis (Fig. 2d) shows that G-MOF-5 have unchanged elemental compositions of C1s, N1s, O1s, Zn 2p1/2, and Zn 2p3/2 located at ca. 285.0, 400.5, 531.5, 1021.3, and 1046.2 eV, respectively, indicating GQDs are well coordinate with metal clusters [Zn4O]6+, similar as the coordination between H2BDC and [Zn4O]6+ in MOF-5. Furthermore, the coordination state of Zn in G-MOF-5 is in well accordance with that in MOF-5 (Fig. 2e), proving that the compatible GQDs are completely incorporated into the MOF-5 scaffold. The XPS C1s spectra (Fig. 2f) can be divided into three peaks located at around 284.6, 286.1, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to the CC, CO, and CO components, respectively. Due to the enriched CO and CO in GQDs (12 and 22 at.%) [41], G-MOF-5 possesses higher CO (16.6 at.%) and CO contents (31.3 at.%) than MOF-5 (12.8 and 27 at.%, Table S1).

After pyrolysis, MOF-5 and G-MOF-5 are converted to MPC and GMPCs, respectively. Taking the optimized GMPC-0.35 as an exemplification, the SEM images indicate that the two samples inherit the similar block appearance as the precursors (Figure S2). However, the inner structure observed from the HRTEM images are quite different. MPC shows a loose inner structure with inhomogeneous mesopores because decomposition of pure MOF-5 precursor causes the serious structure collapse and pore shrinkage (Fig. 3 a). The zoomed image (Fig. 3b) shows that MPC has a typical amorphous carbon structure with abundant micropores. By contrast, the GMPC-0.35 blocks have uniformly distributed mesopores with much smaller pore size than those in MPC (Fig. 3c). Noticeably, some highly crystallized regions are uniformly distributed in the amorphous carbon matrix (Fig. 3d), which can be further confirmed from the similar chemical composition of MPC and GMPC-0.35 by the XPS results (Figure S3 and Table S1), revealing the formation of rigid support by the introduced GQDs.

To reveal the inner support effect of GQDs in MOF-5 derived porous carbons, GMPCs with different mass ratio of GQDs and H2BDC were prepared. The different porous structures and specific surface areas of the samples are presented by the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig. 4 a). Obviously, the samples exhibit a combined type I/IV isotherms, indicating the presence of micro- and meso-porous structure (Fig. 4b). It is noticed that, compared with MPC, GMPCs show higher N2 adsorption volume at the low relative pressure (P/P0 < 0.05) and an obvious larger hysteresis loop at the high relative pressure regions (P/P0 > 0.50), demonstrating a significant improvement of both total and meso- pore volumes from 0.90 and 0.59 cm3 g−1 of MPC to 1.87 and 1.62 cm3 g−1 of GMPC-0.35 through introducing GQDs as inner support. Therefore, GMPC-0.1 and GMPC-0.35 show much higher specific surface area of 1876 m2 g−1 and 1841 m2 g−1, respectively, than MPC (1358 m2 g−1, Fig. 4c, Table S2). As a comparison, the improved surface area of GMPC-0.35 through constructing inner support by GQDs is higher than that of reported modified porous MOF-derived carbons by outside-supported, introducing the sacrificial additional carbon sources, and other nanocarbon supports [24,28,29,47-50] (Table S3). Unfortunately, excessive GQD dosage results in the decreased surface area of GMPC-0.5 (1366 m2 g−1). This is because the large surface area and abundant pores are mainly generated by pyrolysis of MOF-5. The specific surface area of directly pyrolyzed GQDs is only 43 m2 g−1 (Figure S4).

Another interesting thing is that MPC exhibits a two-region pore size distribution with the micropores ranging from 0.7 to 1.3 nm and large meso- and macro-pores with the size beyond 20 nm (Fig. 4b). Differently, GMPCs samples contain abundant mesopores range from 2 to 20 nm instead of the two-region distribution. For pure MOF-5, decomposition of the organic ligands during pyrolysis causes serious pore collapse and shrinking, resulting in the conversion of small mesopores to micro- and macro-pores in MPC. As seen, the micropore volume of MPC (0.31 cm3 g−1) is slightly higher than those of GMPCs (0.19–0.26 cm3 g−1). The uniformly embedded GQDs in G-MOF-5 precursors play as the rigid support, which significantly buffer the structure collapse of the confined MOF-5 microcrystals. Therefore, GMPC-0.35 shows the highest mesopore volume (1.62 cm3 g−1), nearly three times higher than MPC.

Apart from the strong support function, the embedded GQDs also construct inner conductive networks in the MOF-5 derived porous carbons because of their highly crystallized feature [51]. The XRD patterns of the MPC and GMPCs exhibit weak and dispersive (002) and (100) diffraction peaks located at ca. 24° and 43°, respectively (Fig. 4d). The (002) peaks of the samples are gradually intensified with the increase of GQD dosage, indicating an improved crystallinity. The Raman spectra of MPC and GMPCs are fitted into four Gaussian peaks located at ca. 1150, 1345, 1500, and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to the T-, D-, D’-, and G-bands, respectively (Fig. 4e and Figure S5) [40,52]. The area ratios of D- and G-band (AD/AG) monotonically decrease from 1.42 of MPC to 1.12 of GMPC-0.5, indicating the decreased defect density also owing to the highly crystallized GQDs. Therefore, the electrical conductivity greatly increases from 60.7 S m−1 of MPC to 112.5 S m−1 of GMPC-0.35, and 134.6 S m−1 of GMPC-0.5 (Fig. 4f, Figure S6), which is important for electrochemical processes.

The electrochemical performance of the as-prepared samples is measured in three-electrode system. The CV curves at 10 mV s−1 and GCD profiles at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 5 a, b) of the MPC and GMPCs exhibit approximate rectangular and isosceles triangular shapes, respectively, indicating their primary electric double-layer behavior. GMPCs all show larger integral CV area and longer discharge time than MPC (Figure S7 and S8), due to the inner support by GQDs, high surface area, hierarchical porous structure, and interconnected conductivity network. As a result, MPC shows capacitances of 157, 135, and 105 F g−1 at 1, 10, and 50 A g−1, respectively. The optimal sample GMPC-0.35 shows the highest capacitances of 200, 173, and 140 F g−1 at 1, 10, and 50 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 5c). Even at high scan rate of 200 mV s−1, the CV curves of the GMPC-0.35 still shows rectangular shape, revealing robust electrochemical kinetics. In addition, GMPC-0.35 shows a good rate performance with a high capacitance retention of 53% at 100 A g−1.

The improved capacitive and rate performance of GMPCs is due to the novel GQD supported structure, as revealed by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and corresponding quantitative analysis (Fig. 5d and Figure S9). The mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 5f. First, the specific surface areas of GMPCs are significantly improved by the compatible and rigid GQD support, providing abundant active sites for ion storage. Next, thanks to the improved electrical conductivity by embedding high-crystallized GQDs, the internal electrical resistance (Rs) decreases from 0.86 Ω of MPC to 0.63 Ω of GMPC-0.35, and further 0.60 Ω of GMPC-0.5, which facilitates efficient active sites utilization and high rate performance. Moreover, the formation of abundant mesoporous structure by GQD support enables fast ion transport inside the GMPCs. As seen from the Nyquist plots, the diameters of the semicircles in the high frequency region are gradually decreased by introducing GQDs, demonstrating the reduced charge-transfer resistance (Rct). The optimized GMPC-0.35 exhibits the lowest Rct value of 0.13 Ω and Zw value of 0.95 Ω, almost two times lower than MPC (0.25 Ω and 2.24 Ω). The Warburg resistance (Zw) are also significantly decreased. Therefore, GMPC-0.35 shows the best capacitive and rate performance among the as-prepared samples. Increasing the GQD dosage can further improve the electric conductivity of 134.6 S m−1 for GMPC-0.5, but cause the decreased surface area and some blocked ion transport channels. Therefore, GMPC-0.5 shows increased Rct, Zw values (0.14 and 1.15 Ω) and the decreased capacitive and rate performance.

As another evidence, GMPC-0.5 (1366 m2 g−1) shows a similar specific surface area as MPC (1358 m2 g−1). But the capacitive performance and electrochemical kinetics of GMPC-0.5 are better than those of MPC, further proving the advantages of GQD induced mesoporous structure and entire conductive network. The improved electrochemical kinetics by the novel GQD supported structure enables a fast response of the electrode. From the Bode plots given in Fig. 5e, the relaxation time constant (τ0) decreases from 2.08 s of MPC to 0.94 s of GMPC-0.35.

Finally, we assembled GMPC-0.35//GMPC-0.35 supercapacitor to illustrate the application performance. The rectangular shape of CV curves and isosceles triangular shapes of GCD curves (Fig. 6 a and Figure S10) indicate the primary electric double-layer behavior of GMPC-0.35 with good reversibility and fast charge–discharge capability. GMPC-0.35//GMPC-0.35 supercapacitor shows a smaller IR drop of only 0.3 V at 100 A g−1 (Fig. 6b), revealing it good power ability. According to Nyquist plots and the fitted line based the equivalent circuit (inset) depicted in Figure S11, GMPC-0.35//GMPC-0.35 supercapacitor shows lower resistances (0.52 Ω for Rs and 0.09 Ω for Rct) and better capacitive nature compared to the MPC//MPC supercapacitor (0.82 Ω for Rs and 0.21 Ω for Rct). As an important index for supercapacitors, the energy and power densities are reflected in the Ragone plot (Fig. 6c). GMPC-0.35//GMPC-0.35 supercapacitor exhibits high energy densities of 7.34 and 2.50 Wh kg−1 at the power densities of 124.57 and 5256.25 W kg−1, respectively, which are superior than or comparable to other MOF-5 derived carbon materials [50,53-55]. Moreover, GMPC-0.35//GMPC-0.35 supercapacitor show excellent cycle stability without capacity fading after cycling 10,000 times at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 6d).

In summary, we have developed an inner support strategy to prepare MOF-derived porous carbon with high surface area using GQDs as compatible and rigid inner support. The uniformly embedded GQDs effective suppress the structure collapse and pore shrinkage during pyrolysis, leading to significantly improved specific surface area from 1358 to 1841 m2 g−1 and mesopore volume from 0.59 to 1.62 cm3 g−1. The mesoporous structure provides abundant ion transport channels inside the GMPCs. Moreover, the highly-crystallized GQDs construct inner conductive networks, contributing to an improved conductivity from 60.7 to 112.5 S m−1. Owing to the above merits, the optimized GMPC-0.35 shows high specific capacitance of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and good rate performance with the capacitance retention of 53% at 100 A g−1 as electrode material for supercapacitors, demonstrating the great practical application value.

Tingting Tang: Writing – original draft. Renlu Yuan: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Nannan Guo: Methodology. Jiayao Zhu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Xuemeng Gan: Formal analysis. Qiqi Li: Methodology. Fuwei Qin: Formal analysis. Wanxia Luo: Methodology. Luxiang Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology. Su Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Huaihe Song: Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision. Dianzeng Jia: Formal analysis, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52062046, 51702275, and 51672021), the Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), the Research Innovation Project of Postgraduate students in Xinjiang Autonomous Region (XJ2020G008), and the Xinjiang University Doctoral Research Foundation (XJUBSCX-201910).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.04.161.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Porosity engineering of MOF-based materials for electrochemical energy storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Ran, Y. Wu, Y. Yang, T. Zhai, T. Zhou, Q. Shang, L. Zhu, C. Shang, Z. Guo, Porosity engineering of MOF‐based materials for electrochemical energy storage, Adv. Energy. Mater. 11 (2021) 2100154.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in metal-organic framework-based supercapacitor electrode materials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-organic frameworks for energy storage devices: Batteries and supercapacitors
